Thanks for the proposal. That level is well above where we can make this order work on our side, even allowing for current market firmness, so I need to reset the discussion closer to a workable procurement range. I can open at 2.18 per unit for this order today, and if there is a meaningful volume or term commitment attached, we can keep talking about how to structure something that narrows the gap. The supplier opened extremely high relative to our local economics, so accepting is not viable. Rejecting would end the conversation too early; a low but credible counteroffer tests flexibility and establishes our buying range while preserving room to move later.

I appreciate you putting a number on the table, but 2.18 per unit is far below a workable supply level for us, especially against current market conditions. To keep the conversation moving, I can revise from my opening position and offer 6.10 per unit for this order today. If you can pair that with a meaningful volume commitment or some term certainty, I may be able to look at a bit more flexibility from there. The buyer's offer is below a reasonable sell level from our side, so accepting is not realistic. At the same time, the latest market reference is strong, which supports holding materially above the buyer's range while still showing movement from our prior offer to test whether they are serious.

A modest increase signals seriousness without sacrificing too much margin or revealing our full limit.
